text: The surgeon safely inserts an optical trocar into the abdominal cavity, cleans the scope, and proceeds to place additional trocars for the robotic sleeve gastrectomy.
The DaVinci robotic system is brought into position for docking, preparing for the robotic sleeve gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon sits at the console to begin the robotic sleeve gastrectomy procedure using the DaVinci system.
The robotic system is positioned to begin the sleeve gastrectomy procedure, with the surgical team preparing to manipulate the robotic arms for precise dissection and tissue removal.
The surgeon positions a 36 French bougie inside the stomach to guide the sleeve gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a bipolar vessel sealer to access the lesser sac, preparing the area for further dissection during the robotic sleeve gastrectomy.
The surgeon begins the robotic sleeve gastrectomy by creating a window at the inferior portion of the stomach, preparing to work distally along the gastric tissue.
The surgeon continues the dissection, advancing between four and six centimeters from the pylorus, using robotic instruments to precisely manipulate the tissue.
The surgeon points out the pylorus, indicating its location in the surgical field.
The surgeon positions the dissection approximately six centimeters from the pylorus, focusing on the inferior portion of the stomach.
The surgeon identifies the insessura as the main anatomical landmark during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a bipolar vessel sealer to dissect the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon mobilizes a section of the stomach in preparation for creating the sleeve, ensuring adequate separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the stomach, ensuring complete separation from surrounding structures to facilitate the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the stomach, ensuring thorough separation of tissues to prevent the formation of a posterior pouch at the top of the stomach.
The surgeon exposes the left diaphragm, improving visibility beyond the short gastric vessels.
The surgeon has fully exposed the left diaphragm, revealing its anatomical structure adjacent to the mobilized stomach.
The surgeon has completed the dissection of the posterior attachments of the stomach, ensuring full mobilization of the organ.
The surgeon positions the bougie along the stomach to guide the creation of the gastric sleeve, preparing for the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins stapling the stomach from the selected spot, moving upwards, with the left diaphragm fully exposed in the background.
The surgeon prepares to use a blue staple load, likely positioning it for stapling along the stomach or sleeve during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon clamps the stapler onto the stomach tissue, allowing time for edema to reduce before engaging the stapler to create a staple line.
The surgeon prepares to complete the sleeve gastrectomy using blue stapler loads, ensuring the bougie is in position to guide the stapling process.
The surgeon applies compression to the tissue before firing the stapler to secure the staple line.
The surgeon uses a white stapler at the top of the stomach, ensuring no excess pouching is left in the tissue.
The surgeon approaches the diaphragm during the gastrectomy procedure, indicating proximity to this anatomical structure.
The surgeon observes the formation of the gastric sleeve, noting its well-formed appearance after stapling.
The surgeon exposes the hiatus, the opening in the diaphragm through which the esophagus passes, during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully trims the gastric tissue to avoid leaving excess tissue, known as a 'dog ear,' ensuring a smooth and even formation of the sleeve during the gastrectomy.
The surgical team adjusts the operating table to a fifteen-degree angle in preparation for performing a leak test.
The surgeon removes the bougie from the gastric sleeve, ensuring the sleeve maintains its shape without excess tissue or 'dog ear' formation.
The surgeon applies hemostatic measures at the top of the surgical site to control minor bleeding.
The surgeon applies fibrin sealant to the surgical site, ensuring coverage over the area to promote hemostasis and tissue adhesion.
The surgeon removes the trocar and uses a Kelly clamp to dilate the hole created by the trocar insertion.
